summary: This benchmark evaluates a first-principles prompt kernel against traditional patch rules in an agentic movie workflow. The study demonstrates that a compact reasoning layer can maintain agent behavior across various workflows without relying on numerous specific keyword triggers, suggesting a more robust prompt engineering strategy. The methodology is sound, but the reliance on internal metrics and a specific GPT model limits broader applicability.
strengths: Rigorous methodology using a no-media agent-flow harness that imports production components.
Clear articulation of the benchmark question and the variants tested.
Practical assessment lessons derived from the experiment.
Addresses a relevant problem in prompt engineering: managing complexity and maintainability.
weaknesses: Reliance on internal rubric scores and private run artifacts, hindering external reproducibility.
The benchmark is specific to GPT-5.5, potentially limiting generalizability to other models.
The novelty is moderate, as prompt engineering strategies are an active area of research.
suggestions: Provide more details on the 'first-principles reasoning kernel' and how it's implemented.
Discuss potential strategies for making the benchmark more reproducible, perhaps by defining standardized internal metrics or providing sample data structures.
Explore the performance of the first-principles kernel with other LLMs to assess its model-agnosticism.

claims.text: The first-principles variant did not regress the representative suite: first-shot smoke scored 98.3 A+, simple edit preserved structure with one targeted update, and Canvas workflows stayed scoped to board operations.
claims.source: The article body describes these results in detail. While internal metrics, the claims are consistent with the described methodology.

claims.text: Live GPT-5.5 runs compared the production baseline, a first-principles variant, and a targeted product-promo guard across first-shot movie creation, simple edits, and Canvas Studio board workflows.
The harness imports the same planner, phase executor, action parser, dispatcher, prompt registry, and project-context builder used by the app.

novelty_vs_existing.title: Prompt Compression Benchmark for Agentic Movie Generation: 75 Percent Smaller Prompts with Quality Caveats
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: This submission focuses on a different aspect of prompt engineering: replacing narrow patch rules with a first-principles reasoning kernel, rather than prompt compression. It complements the existing work by exploring a different strategy for managing prompt complexity and maintainability.

novelty_vs_existing.title: A Prompt Autoresearch Loop for Agent Systems with Manifest Provenance
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: While both deal with agent systems and prompts, this submission is a benchmark comparing two approaches (kernel vs. patches), whereas the related paper focuses on an autoresearch loop for prompt improvement. This work provides empirical evidence for a specific prompt design philosophy.

novelty_vs_existing.title: Auditing Prompt Runtime Contract Drift in Tool-Using Creative Agents
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: This submission is a benchmark that provides data on how a first-principles kernel affects agent behavior, which can inform auditing strategies. The existing paper focuses on detecting drift, while this work proposes a method to potentially reduce drift by design.
